constraints: Keep routine loot currency shops unsecured rewards banking and permanent progression personal
Grant every eligible player a strongbox simultaneously for major authored rewards
Roll each granted strongbox rarity independently
Prevent loot stealing griefing and permanent ownership disputes
Reward active connected players
Reward life-exhausted players who remain connected as spectators
Do not reward players who permanently left the live session
Defer optional reconnect grace handling until online implementation
Use mission identity difficulty and explicit challenge settings for the base reward table
Use only the reward owner's account level as the personal progression input
Never let teammate level gear performance or progression modify another player's odds
Support mixed-level friends without a carry altering the newer player's rarity table
Use one selected mission difficulty for all players
Do not normalize player power or scale from account levels
Allow experienced players to carry newer friends
Allow later level-capped equal-level or standardized-loadout challenges
Keep valid co-op loot currency experience and account rewards
Do not count a co-op clear as a solo clear
Track co-op difficulty challenge and record state separately
Make the distinction explicit in results and campaign interfaces
Unlock later co-op missions through eligible co-op clears
Leave solo campaign progression unchanged
Allow groups to progress together without mandatory solo homework
Show solo and co-op unlock and completion state distinctly
Allow the party leader to launch missions unlocked on the leader's co-op track
Mark less-progressed participants visibly as guests
Let guests retain personal loot account rewards and valid guest records
Advance guest co-op campaign only when personal prerequisites are satisfied
Show progression eligibility before launch
Use activated teleports as the controlled eventual entry point for new late joiners
Do not spawn late joiners directly into combat
Allow the first co-op MVP to use lobby-only entry plus reconnect support
Add teleport late join only after synchronization rewards lives and reconnects are reliable
Reduce late-join lives according to reached teleport segments or comparable milestones
Use a simple understandable authored rule
Do not derive the allowance from teammate account levels or complex remaining-life averages
Playtest carefully against unfair entry and fresh-player rotation exploits
Defer exact counts and steps
Make three-player and four-player encounters meaningfully different
Scale from active player count and mission settings rather than account levels
Use restrained encounter budget reinforcement enemy count durability or composition adjustments
Lock party-size scaling when the room encounter begins
Prefer added pressure and composition over excessive health sponginess
Defer exact coefficients to prototypes and playtests

open_questions_added: Final supported co-op party-size target and staged rollout
Network topology and host-migration strategy for later co-op
Disconnected-player reward and reconnection grace policy
Account-level contribution to personal strongbox rarity
Co-op matchmaking and party privacy modes
Solo versus co-op achievement and leaderboard policy
Co-op enemy targeting and room-entry synchronization rules

next_action.instruction: Ask exactly one concise A/B/C decision card selecting whether future co-op is capped at two players, targets up to four players but may stage the first implementation around two-player validation, or requires full four-player support from the first co-op MVP. Preserve post-MVP co-op status, fixed mission difficulty, active-player-count encounter scaling, personal non-replenishable difficulty-based lives, spectator clutch play, separate co-op progression, host continuity, teleport-based late joining as a later goal, solo-lead production, and Unity with C#. Include social appeal, encounter readability, projectile and effect density, camera and room constraints, UI, network bandwidth and synchronization, host migration, testing combinations, matchmaking population, implementation cost, and risk of architecture that cannot later expand. Put the recommendation after all A/B/C options at the end of the card. Queue D-191 through D-200 and persist at D-200 unless the user changes the batching rule or requests a context handoff.
